findings.detail: doImport logs `console.error('Import row failed:', err?.message, data)` where `data` is the full mapped customer object (name, email, phone, address, notes). Same pattern logs customer.name at lines 1335 and 1364. If Sentry/PostHog/console-piping is ever added (and it's standard in SaaS to forward console errors), this becomes a third-country data export. Even without, shared-screen support sessions or developer-tools screenshots leak the data.
findings.fix: Log only the row index and err.message; never log payload fields. If sampling is needed for debugging, hash the email and redact phone/address.

findings.title: Customer name, notes and service history sent to AI generation endpoint without consent capture
findings.detail: generateAIMessage interpolates customer.name, customer.notes, services, and dates into a prompt and posts to the `ai-generate` edge function (presumably Anthropic). Free-text notes commonly contain special-category data (health info, vulnerable adult notes, key holder details). UK GDPR requires (a) a DPA with the processor (Anthropic — check), (b) appropriate lawful basis for processing through a third country, (c) a privacy notice telling customers their data is sent to an AI vendor. There is no UI surface telling the operator or end customer.
findings.fix: Add a per-business AI feature toggle (off by default), redact common patterns (keysafe digits, health terms) before send, and update the privacy notice to disclose the processor.

findings.title: PII import wizard has no source-consent attestation or audit record
findings.detail: The wizard accepts CSV/XLSX of names, emails, phones, addresses, balances (lines 7-12, 19-41) and bulk upserts via upsertCustomer without recording the lawful basis, source, or the date of consent. UK GDPR Article 30 requires controllers to document processing activities — and where the import is from a competing vendor (Squeegee, CleanerPlanner, Aworka) the operator should attest they have rights to migrate. Imports also do not create a `data_provenance` record for SAR responses ('where did you get my data from').
findings.fix: Add a 1-click attestation step in the wizard storing { source, attested_by_user_id, attested_at, file_hash, row_count } into a new `customer_import_audits` table with business-scoped RLS. Surface in privacy/SAR flow.

findings.title: pending_customers stored on server for over-cap free users without retention policy
findings.detail: When a Lite user exceeds LITE_CAP=30 customers, the overflow PII is stored via storePendingCustomers (line 1441) into pending_customers table (migration 008 line 152). There is no documented retention/auto-purge schedule; a user who never upgrades leaves customer PII parked indefinitely. UK GDPR Article 5(1)(e) (storage limitation) requires a defined retention.
findings.fix: Add a Postgres scheduled job (or trigger) that deletes pending_customers rows older than e.g. 90 days, and disclose the retention in the upgrade-cap modal.

findings.title: No CSV-injection sanitisation on import
findings.detail: Imported fields (name, notes, address) are accepted verbatim and stored. If an operator later exports customers to CSV (no export exists yet but is required for SAR/Article 20), values starting with =, +, -, @ become formula-injection payloads in Excel. No risk to current XSS surface (React escapes by default and no dangerouslySetInnerHTML was found), but worth pre-empting for the export feature.
findings.fix: Prefix any cell starting with =, +, -, @ with a single quote in CSV export; alternatively reject those characters at the start of imported text fields.

findings.title: Natural-language customer search & filter ('show me lapsed residentials in BS7 worth >£500')
findings.detail: Trigger: a sparkle button inside the existing search bar (line ~2380 area where search/filter state lives) toggles 'Ask Cadi' mode. User types free-form: 'commercial customers I haven't seen in 6 months who paid by DD' or 'top 10 by LTV in Bath area'. Claude (Haiku) returns a structured filter spec {status, segment, postcodePrefix, ltvMin, lastJobBefore, sort} which the existing client-side filter pipeline applies — no new query layer needed. Wow: ServiceM8/Jobber force users to memorise 6 dropdown combos; Cadi turns the whole filter bar into a one-line prompt and chains across segment+postcode+LTV+recency in ways the current UI can't even express. Compounds: same NL-filter shell can be reused on Jobs, Invoices, Staff. Data: customers only (already in memory).
findings.fix: Add NL→filter-spec edge function call; map JSON spec onto existing useMemo filter chain around line 2390.

findings.title: Churn / at-risk prediction with reasoning, not rules
findings.detail: Trigger: replaces/augments the 'urgency' sort and the rules-based generateSuggestions() block at lines 47–190. A nightly batch (or on-open) sends each customer a compact JSON profile {frequency, lastJobDays, avgInterval, cancellations, paymentLateness, ratingTrend, notesText} to Sonnet. Output: churnRisk 0-1 + 1-sentence reason ('Skipped last 2 fortnightly slots and notes mention moving house — likely churning'). Surfaces as a red Chip on customer card and feeds the 'urgent' sort. Wow: current engine is hardcoded thresholds; Sonnet reads the free-text notes field (which contains the actual signal — 'mentioned baby due', 'house on the market') that no rules engine can parse. Massive moat vs Jobber/ServiceM8. Data: customers + jobs + invoices + notes. Cost: batch once/day at ~1k tokens each = pennies per 1000 customers.
findings.fix: New edge function ai-churn-score; cache score+reason on customers table (add columns churn_score, churn_reason, churn_scored_at).

findings.title: Smart customer dedupe & merge during import
findings.detail: Trigger: step 3 (preview) of the import wizard. Before commit, Haiku is sent the incoming rows + a candidate list of existing customers matched by fuzzy name/postcode. Returns: {action: 'merge'|'create'|'skip', mergeTargetId, mergedFields, reason} per row. UI shows 'We think Sarah Jones, 12 Oak Rd = existing Sarah J. at 12 Oak Road — merge?'. Wow: importing from CleanerPlanner/Squeegee/Aworka is the #1 churn killer when switching providers — competitors dump duplicates and the user spends 3 days cleaning up. Cadi does it in 30 seconds. Data: existing customers list + import rows. Pair with column-mapper auto-detect (same call).
findings.fix: Inject AI dedup pass between mapping (step 3) and commit (step 4) in CustomerImport.jsx.

findings.title: Auto-column-mapping for unknown CSV formats
findings.detail: Trigger: when source = 'Other / unknown' or detected headers don't match a known template. Haiku gets the header row + 3 sample rows + the target schema (name, address, postcode, phone, email, frequency, price, last_job_date). Returns a mapping JSON + confidence. Wow: today the user has to manually map 12 columns from a weird CSV; with Haiku it's pre-filled with 95% accuracy. Even Squeegee doesn't do this. Data: just the parsed CSV in memory. Reusable on Jobs/Invoices import.
findings.fix: Add a 'Suggest mapping' button in step 3 that calls ai-generate with a tight JSON schema prompt.

findings.title: Conversational customer creation ('Add Mrs Patel, 14 Elm St BS8, fortnightly £25, key in vault')
findings.detail: Trigger: '+ Add customer' button opens a chat input INSTEAD of the modal (mirrors the ServiceChat pattern already shipped per memory). One sentence in → fully populated customer + first scheduled job + vault entry. Sonnet parses to structured fields, asks 1 clarifying Q if needed, then offers 'Book first clean Tues?'. Wow: matches the conversational-onboarding voice of the product. Cuts add-customer from 90s of form-filling to one sentence. Voice input on mobile = unbeatable on the van. Data: customers, customer_rounds, jobs, secure_vault tables. Compounds with ServiceChat & SchedulerChat.
findings.fix: Reuse ServiceChat scaffold; new edge function ai-parse-customer.

findings.title: Per-customer upsell briefing card (uses Services tab catalogue)
findings.detail: Trigger: 'Suggestions' tab in customer detail panel (line ~1874). Today it shows rules-based generic suggestions. Replace with Sonnet call that joins this customer's job history + notes + segment with the user's actual Services catalogue (oven clean £55, gutter clear £85, etc.) and produces 2-3 ranked upsells with cash value and the exact opener line ('Hi Sarah, noticed it's been 8 months since your last oven clean — £55, slot Thurs?'). Wow: rules engine doesn't know what services the business actually offers; this does. The message is one tap away (already wired to MessageComposer). Data: customers + jobs + services + notes. Compounds Customers × Services × Schedule.
findings.fix: New ai-customer-upsell edge function; surface in existing Suggestions tab.

findings.title: Notes summariser & relationship memory
findings.detail: Trigger: top of customer Overview tab — a 'Relationship summary' callout. Haiku takes the running notes field + last 10 job notes + complaint history and returns a 2-line digest: 'Mrs Patel — prefers Tues mornings, allergic to bleach, husband works nights (be quiet), tipped at Christmas. Last issue: missed bin day Apr 2026 — resolved.' Wow: the notes field today is a wall of text nobody re-reads; this turns institutional knowledge into a glance. Critical for Britannia's 1500-staff scale where the cleaner today is not the cleaner last month. Data: customers.notes + jobs.notes. Cache in customers.ai_summary, regenerate on note change.
findings.fix: Edge function ai-customer-summary; trigger on note save (debounced).

findings.title: Win-back campaign builder (multi-customer batch)
findings.detail: Trigger: when 'lapsed' filter is active, a 'Win back all (N)' button appears above the list. Sonnet generates personalised win-back messages for each lapsed customer in one batch call, grouped by why they likely lapsed (price-sensitive, scheduling clash, complaint, ghosted). User reviews in a table, edits any, hits 'Send all' (SMS/email). Wow: every CRM has a 'send template' button; nobody segments the WHY of lapsing or personalises by job history. Cadi turns a dead list into £££. Data: lapsed customers, last job, last invoice, any complaint notes. Compounds with Messaging tab.
findings.fix: Bulk edge function ai-winback-batch; new modal mounted from Customers list toolbar.

findings.title: Predicted next-clean & 'price is wrong' detector
findings.detail: Trigger: small AI badge on customer card if either (a) their stated frequency doesn't match actual job cadence ('says fortnightly, actually 23-day average — drift') or (b) their price is >15% below median for similar properties in the user's book. Sonnet (or even deterministic stats + Haiku for the message) flags it on the overview tab with a 'Review pricing' CTA. Wow: cleaners systematically undercharge long-term customers because raising prices feels awkward — Cadi gives them the data + the script. This is real money found in seconds. Data: customers + jobs + invoices (all in DB). Compounds with Pricing/Services and the upcoming AI Pricing engine.
findings.fix: Batch job nightly; store flags on customers; render badge in card render path.

findings.title: Secure-vault entry parsing from photo / paste
findings.detail: Trigger: in the Secure tab, 'Add from photo/text' alongside the manual form. User pastes 'Keysafe round side of garage, code 4471, dog is friendly but barks, bin day Weds black' or uploads a photo of a note. Sonnet (vision) extracts {accessType, code, location, hazards, binDay, petInfo} into structured vault rows. Wow: cleaners get access info via WhatsApp/scribbled notes — nobody types it in cleanly. This makes vault adoption frictionless. Data: secure_vault table; Sonnet with vision. Reusable on Onboarding ('paste the welcome pack').
findings.fix: Add upload/paste affordance to vault tab; route through ai-generate with vision.

findings.title: Add button skips the catalogue picker entirely — forces every new user into a chat
findings.detail: `openAdd = () => setChatSession({ context: 'add_new' })` means the prominent top-right "Add a service" button (line 1254) AND the dashed "Add a service" card (line 1336) and the empty-state CTA (line 1321) all launch ServiceChat. The Catalogue picker (CatalogueModal) is only reachable via the chat's "Use form instead" link, which then routes to `setCatalogueOpen(true)` (line 1393). A cleaner who already knows they want "Oven Clean, Carpet Clean, Inside Windows" has to type each one in a chat conversation rather than tapping three chips. Real friction: chat is slower for known-quantity tasks. Offer a split button ("Chat with Cadi" vs "Pick from list") or open catalogue first with a "Build it with Cadi" secondary action.
findings.fix: Make first interaction a chooser: catalogue + chat side by side, or a split button on the header.

findings.title: First-visit + onboarding seed + attention banner can stack and dominate the viewport
findings.detail: On a brand-new account with onboarding services seeded, the user could see: (1) the first-visit blue banner (line 1264), (2) the amber pricing-attention banner (line 1277), and (3) the auto-opened ServiceChat modal (line 1125) — all on first paint. The chat covers the page so they never see the banners until they close it, then get hit with both. Sequence them, or suppress the banners while chat is open.
findings.fix: Hide the two banners while chatSession is non-null; show a single contextual banner after chat closes.

findings.title: "Edit with Cadi" is the only edit option in the card menu — no fast inline edit
findings.detail: The card kebab menu has only "Edit with Cadi", Duplicate, Delete (lines 981-989). To change a single price tier, a cleaner must enter a chat, navigate to edit_menu, pick Pricing, retype the entire matrix. There's no "Edit fields" (form) shortcut on the card. Add a second item "Quick edit" that opens the form modal directly, or surface price-cells as click-to-edit inline on the card.
findings.fix: Add 'Quick edit' option in kebab menu that opens ServiceModal in edit mode; or inline-editable price chip.

findings.title: Pricing matrix has no per-frequency multiplier — a real cleaner's biggest unmet need
findings.detail: DEFAULT_SIZE_TIERS captures one price per bedroom, and Frequency is just checkboxes for what's allowed. Real UK domestic cleaners almost always charge differently for weekly vs fortnightly vs one-off (deep clean premium). The schema supports `pricing_matrix` but there's no UI to say "2 bed weekly £45, 2 bed fortnightly £55, 2 bed one-off £75". Front Desk will quote the same price regardless of frequency — losing margin on one-offs and undercutting on regulars. PricingSettings.jsx has a FreqModifiersEditor but it lives in a separate module and isn't joined to the per-service matrix.
findings.fix: Add a frequency multiplier toggle in the size matrix (e.g. one-off +50%, fortnightly +20%) inline in the Services form.

findings.title: Locked "Profit margin tracking — unlock with Pro" on every card is misleading
findings.detail: Memory says pricing is single tier £29/mo, no Pro tier. The lock-with-Pro tease is a dead end — clicking does nothing, and there's no Pro tier to upgrade to. This will confuse and annoy new users immediately on the most prominent card real estate.
findings.fix: Remove the locked teaser, or wire it to a 'coming soon' affordance / waitlist toggle. If it's marketing-only, link it to a feature roadmap page.

findings.title: No undo on delete despite an irreversible warning
findings.detail: DeleteModal warns "This can't be undone" (line 904) and `deleteService` is called immediately on confirm (line 1203). The toast just says "X removed" with no undo action. A common SaaS pattern is a 5-second "Undo" toast that soft-deletes. For a small business owner who may delete the wrong service while bulk-cleaning their menu, this is genuinely dangerous data loss.
findings.fix: Implement soft delete (deleted_at column) with a 5-10s Undo action button on the toast.

findings.title: Toast component lacks ARIA live region and dismiss control
findings.detail: The Toast component (line 1067) has no `role="status"` / `aria-live="polite"` so screen readers won't announce "X added to your menu". It also auto-dismisses at 3.5s with no manual close or pause. Users on mobile with cognitive or reading delays will miss key confirmations like the import message at line 1127.
findings.fix: Add role="status" aria-live="polite", a close button, and pause-on-hover.

findings.title: Radio-style pricing picker uses fake radios with sr-only inputs and no keyboard group semantics
findings.detail: PRICING_TYPES are rendered as <label> wrapping a custom div + sr-only radio (line 659). The whole row is clickable but tab focus lands on the hidden radio with no visible focus ring (since it's `sr-only`). A keyboard-only user can't see which option is focused. Also no `role="radiogroup"` on the container.
findings.fix: Add visible focus styles tied to :focus-visible on the visual circle, wrap in role=radiogroup with aria-labelledby.

findings.title: Checkbox replacement <button> for frequency options has no aria-pressed or role
findings.detail: Frequency "checkboxes" are <button> elements (line 747) with no `aria-pressed` or `role="checkbox"` + `aria-checked`. Screen reader users hear "button" with no indication of state. Same pattern repeats for Active toggle (line 834), Site visit toggle (line 855), service area toggle (line 770).
findings.fix: Add role=switch + aria-checked for the toggles, role=checkbox + aria-checked for the multi-select.

findings.title: NumberInput allows step=0.01 but provides no decimal-keyboard hints on mobile
findings.detail: `<input type="number" step="0.01">` on iOS Safari brings up the number pad without a decimal key. UK cleaners on phones will struggle to type £45.50. Use `inputMode="decimal"` and `pattern="[0-9]*\.?[0-9]*"`. Also no thousands separator on the display, and putting price 1234 vs 12.34 is easy to fat-finger.
findings.fix: Add inputMode=decimal, pattern, and consider a blur-time format pass.

findings.title: Empty state examples are static text not actionable
findings.detail: EmptyState says "Examples: regular domestic clean, deep clean, end of tenancy" as plain text. These could be tap-to-add chips that pre-fill the chat or catalogue, removing a step. WOW moment opportunity: 'Tap one to add it instantly'.
findings.fix: Render examples as <button> chips that call openCustomAdd with the name prefilled.

findings.title: Service catalogue lacks UK-specific pricing benchmarks — biggest wow moment missed
findings.detail: When a cleaner picks "End of Tenancy" from the catalogue, Cadi knows from training data + Britannia-scale Cadi customers that the UK median is ~£18-22/hr or £180-280 for a 2-bed. The catalogue could show 'Typical UK price: £200 for 2 bed (based on cleaners in your postcode)' next to each chip. No competitor does this. The mapper notes seed creates services with `pricing_type: 'custom'` (line 1167) — every catalogue add is a blank price waiting for the user. Instead, suggest a default based on postcode/region/competitor scrape, which the user accepts or overrides.
findings.fix: Add 'Smart suggest pricing' button after catalogue add: shows regional median + lets user accept/customise.

findings.title: Site-visit toggle copy is good — but no way to write a custom "we'll be in touch" message
findings.detail: The toggle says Front Desk "will collect contact details and request a visit" but there's no field for the message that gets sent or what details to collect. Power-users (commercial cleaners) need to control this — every commercial enquiry needs Sq ft / floors / visit dates etc.
findings.fix: When site_visit_required=true, show a textarea for custom intake questions and an SMS/email template snippet.

findings.title: Description fields are plain text, but inclusions/exclusions deserve list UI
findings.detail: description_included and description_excluded are textareas. ServiceChat already parses these into arrays (parseIncEx, ServiceChat.jsx:164-184) then re-joins as `included.join(', ')` (line 820). A bulleted-list editor would (a) match Front Desk's quote format, (b) prevent the comma-joining mess when a description itself contains a comma (e.g. "hoovering, including under furniture"), and (c) feel more like a checklist a cleaner builds for staff.
findings.fix: Replace textarea with chip/tag editor for inclusions and exclusions — also makes ServiceChat parsing reversible.

findings.title: Quick mode label is invisible / hidden affordance
findings.detail: The Quick/Thorough toggle pill at the top of the chat (line 1223) is small grey text, easy to miss. The user might spend 7 turns answering thorough questions without realising they can flip to quick. Surface this as an explicit choice in the welcome bubble: 'Do you want the quick version (3 questions) or the thorough version (7)?'
findings.fix: Promote mode choice to a welcome-step button selection rather than a hidden header toggle.

findings.title: No 'Save and reuse pricing' — wow moment for setting up multiple sizes once
findings.detail: A residential cleaner with Weekly Clean, Fortnightly Clean, Deep Clean all uses the same 1-bed/2-bed/3-bed size matrix with different prices. Right now they re-enter labels every time. Add a "Use my standard size tiers" template that auto-loads labels (saved once at business profile level), so they only fill in prices. Same for hourly rate inheritance across services.
findings.fix: Persist user's size tier labels and hourly rate as defaults in profile; offer 'Copy pricing from another service' button in form.

findings.title: Front Desk preview button doesn't reveal the magic
findings.detail: "Try Front Desk" is a great moment, but it's just a button labelled the same in every state. Could become a sticky 'Front Desk is ready to quote 5 services 👋' chip after services are added — or animate the customer-facing widget in a corner peek when the user saves their first priced service. This is the moment Cadi proves ROI; right now the user has to remember to click it.
findings.fix: After first priced service saved, auto-show a 2-line peek of Front Desk simulating a customer quote with that service.

findings.title: Modal trap: no focus management on open, no escape key handler
findings.detail: ServiceModal, CatalogueModal, DeleteModal, ServiceChat all open without focusing the close button or first interactive element, and there's no Escape key listener that calls onClose. Keyboard users have to tab through everything underneath to dismiss.
findings.fix: Add focus-trap (useEffect setting focus on mount) and document-level Escape handler in each modal.

findings.title: No bulk operations — can't activate/deactivate/delete multiple services at once
findings.detail: A seasonal cleaner who turns off Gutter Clearance Oct-Mar has to toggle each gutter-related service one at a time. The grid has no checkbox/select-all/bulk-actions row. For a business with 30+ services (Britannia scale), this is hours of work.
findings.fix: Add multi-select mode with bulk active/inactive/delete/category-move.

findings.title: Color contrast on placeholder + helper text likely fails WCAG AA
findings.detail: Helper text uses `text-[rgba(153,197,255,0.5)]` on dark navy bg (line 227) and placeholders use 0.3 alpha (line 238). At 30% opacity on #040810 background these will fail the 4.5:1 contrast ratio required for body text. The subtitle at line 598 (`rgba(153,197,255,0.5)`) is critical context ("This is what Front Desk quotes from") that low-vision users may miss.
findings.fix: Bump opacity to 0.7+ for helper/subtitle and 0.5 minimum for placeholders, verify with contrast checker.

findings.title: Modal scroll-locked body — no overflow handling for very long forms on mobile
findings.detail: ServiceModal uses `max-h-[92vh]` with inner overflow-y-auto. On small phones in landscape, the footer buttons can get pushed below visible area when the body content + accordion expansions exceed available height. No sticky save bar.
findings.fix: Make footer sticky-bottom inside a flex column container or add iOS safe-area-inset padding.

findings.title: localStorage flags will surprise users on shared devices
findings.detail: `cadi_services_chat_opened` and `cadi_services_visited` are written to localStorage, meaning if a cleaner logs out and a different user logs in on the same device, the second user won't see the first-visit banner or auto-chat. For a husband-wife cleaning business sharing an iPad, this is a real bug.
findings.fix: Scope these flags to user_id (use a Set keyed by user id) or store in profiles.setup_data.

findings.title: AI photo analysis is permanently broken (dead-end stub)
findings.detail: analysePhoto() unconditionally throws 'AI photo analysis coming soon'. The entire PhotoCapture component still exists (lines 331-437) with full result-rendering UI for an object shape that can never arrive. The 'Coming soon' placeholder is shown when no preview exists (line 371), but if a user does upload, handle() at line 340 will run, hit the throw, and surface 'Couldn't analyse the photo' (line 349) — making it look like a transient error rather than an unimplemented feature. NOTE: this is in PricingCalculator (used by /Calculator and TrainingStaff), not the Services tab itself.
findings.fix: Either (a) delete PhotoCapture entirely until the edge function is built and just keep the 'Coming soon' static card, or (b) disable the file input so handle() can never run. Do not leave a broken throw behind a try/catch that masks it as a generic failure.

findings.title: PhotoCapture 'Apply AI prices' button has dead-end onClick
findings.detail: `onClick={() => onResult ? () => onResult(result) : undefined}` — when onResult IS defined this returns a function instead of calling it (the outer arrow returns the inner arrow without invoking). So even if AI analysis worked, clicking 'Apply AI prices' would do nothing. Confirmed verbatim.
findings.fix: Replace with `onClick={onResult ? () => onResult(result) : undefined}` and `disabled={!onResult}`. Or just call `onResult?.(result)` directly.

findings.title: Service catalogue duplicated across 4 files with diverging keys
findings.detail: Confirmed. Services.jsx lists user-facing names ('Weekly Clean', 'Deep Clean', 'End of Tenancy'); the three Pricing* files list a separate keyed taxonomy ('regular_clean', 'deep_clean', 'end_of_tenancy_clean'). The two namespaces don't map to each other — a service added via Services.jsx as 'Weekly Clean' has no link to a pricing_rules row keyed 'regular_clean'. PricingSandbox.jsx and PricingConversation.jsx have IDENTICAL ALL_SERVICES arrays (lines 6-29 vs 8-31), pure copy-paste. This is the root data-model fracture in the Services area.
findings.fix: Decide on ONE source of truth. Either (a) drive everything off the services DB table (let users name their own services, key pricing_rules off service.id rather than a static key), or (b) extract `src/lib/catalogue.js` exporting one canonical list and have all four files import from it. PricingConversation.jsx and PricingSandbox.jsx should share the array immediately — that's a 5-minute change with zero risk.

findings.title: Pricing-method/frequency vocabulary duplicated 3× with mismatched keys
findings.detail: Services.jsx PRICING_TYPES uses values per_size/hourly/fixed/per_sqm/per_room/custom. PricingSettings.jsx METHOD_META uses per_bedroom/per_bedroom_bathroom/per_sqm/per_hour/flat_rate_by_size/flat_rate_fixed/site_visit_required — completely different keys. ServiceChat.jsx parsePricing produces yet a third set: per_sqm/hourly/per_size/fixed/custom (Services-style). So a rule created in PricingConversation (per_bedroom) is invisible to Services.jsx's pricing summary code. Frequency keys also diverge: Services/ServiceChat use frequency_one_off/weekly/fortnightly/monthly/quarterly/annually; PricingSettings uses one_off/weekly/fortnightly/four_weekly/monthly (no quarterly/annual; adds four_weekly).
findings.fix: Reconcile to a single enum in `src/lib/pricingMethods.js` and a single frequency enum in `src/lib/frequencies.js`. Add a one-shot migration to remap legacy values. Until then, document that Services.jsx (services table) and PricingSettings.jsx (pricing_rules table) are two separate pricing systems — this should be loud in CLAUDE.md.

findings.title: PricingCalculator.jsx is a 4,415-line god component
findings.detail: Single file contains: profit math (calcProfit, priceForMargin), formatting helpers, the broken AI photo stub, PhotoCapture, ProfitWaterfall, AddOnSection, StaffSelector, SavedQuotesList, QuoteSummary, ResidentialTab (state at lines 813-822 — 10+ useStates), CommercialTab (lines 1274-1289 — 15+ useStates), ExteriorTab (lines 2095-2109 — 13+ useStates), shared Card/Chip/Alert/PillRow primitives, plus GInput/GSection/GColHeader/GCard duplicate primitives. Three tab components each manage their own saved-quotes list, customer search, target margin, hourly rate input — same patterns reimplemented three times.
findings.fix: Split per-tab into src/components/pricing-calculator/ResidentialTab.jsx, CommercialTab.jsx, ExteriorTab.jsx with a shared hooks/usePricingTab.js for the common state (hourlyRate, targetMargin, savedQuotes, customer). Move calcProfit/priceForMargin to src/lib/profitEngine.js. Move shared UI to src/components/ui/. Realistic 2-3 day refactor; do it before adding any more pricing surface.

findings.title: Services.jsx is 1,406 lines — page + 6 sub-components in one file
findings.detail: Contains the Services page, ServiceModal (huge form ~600 lines), DeleteModal, ServiceCard, EmptyState, Toast, plus 4 module-level constant blocks. ServiceModal alone has FREQ_OPTIONS, accordion sections for inclusions/exclusions/pricing notes/frequency/service area/materials/private notes/active toggle/site visit — it's its own page-sized component. The chat flow (ServiceChat) is already extracted; the form should be too.
findings.fix: Extract src/pages/services/ServiceModal.jsx, ServiceCard.jsx, DeleteModal.jsx, EmptyState.jsx, Toast.jsx. Move the constants (CATEGORIES, PRICING_TYPES, DEFAULT_SIZE_TIERS, FREQ_OPTIONS, EMPTY_FORM, SERVICE_CATALOGUE) to src/lib/services/constants.js so they can be imported by both Services.jsx and ServiceChat.jsx (which currently can't see them).

findings.title: ServiceChat.jsx is 1,304 lines with 18+ steps inlined in renderInput
findings.detail: renderInput() is a giant if/else ladder over step states. Every step's input UI, microcopy, and transition logic is jammed into one component. The parsing helpers (parsePricing/extractBedPrices/parseDuration/parseIncEx/parseFrequency/parseNoteCategory at lines 21-200) are pure functions that should be extracted and unit-tested — they're regex-driven parsers for currency/durations/frequencies and have zero tests today.
findings.fix: Extract src/lib/services/chatParsers.js with parsePricing/parseDuration/etc. and add unit tests (these are the highest-bug-risk code in the area — silent misparse = wrong price in user's catalogue). Optionally split renderInput into per-step components in src/components/service-chat/steps/.

findings.title: Services.load() refetches on every user/navigate change with seeding side effect
findings.detail: useEffect(() => { load(); ... }, [load]) and load is `useCallback(..., [navigate, user])`. user from AuthContext is often a new object reference on every auth event/render in many setups — would cause repeat seed attempts. Worse, the seeding branch (lines 1106-1131) hits profiles, calls seedServicesFromOnboarding, re-lists, sets state, and EARLY-RETURNS before setLoading(false) cleanup is reached... actually the finally clause runs, but the early return skips setAttentionCount/setServices(all) — fine. The real bug: if load runs twice concurrently (user updates mid-flight), seedServicesFromOnboarding could be called twice and double-seed.
findings.fix: Add an `isMounted` ref or AbortController; gate the seed branch behind a `seededRef.current` so it can only run once per mount. Depend on `user?.id` only, not the whole user object.

findings.title: Toast pattern swallows error details — all failures map to 'Something went wrong'
findings.detail: handleCatalogueAdd, handleDuplicate, handleDeleteConfirm, handleToggleActive all catch errors and show the same generic toast. The catch in handleToggleActive doesn't even log (line 1218 `catch (e) {`). Users hitting an RLS violation, a network failure, a constraint error all see the same string. The catch at line 1207 also doesn't log. Only handleCatalogueAdd logs to console.
findings.fix: Centralize via `reportError(action, e)` that console.errors with action context AND surfaces a toast. Consider Sentry/PostHog capture — these are silent in production today.

findings.title: PricingConversation save: silent failure on archive query and addons insert
findings.detail: The archive update at line 327-332 has no error check — if it fails (e.g. RLS), the code continues and inserts a new active row, producing TWO active rules for the same service. Same at line 364: `await supabase.from('pricing_addons').insert(addonRows)` — return value is destructured and ignored, errors are silently swallowed inside the same try block that succeeds with setSaved(true).
findings.fix: Destructure { error } from both calls and throw on failure: `const { error: archiveErr } = await supabase.from(...).update(...); if (archiveErr) throw archiveErr;` and same for addons. Or wrap pricing_rule + pricing_addons writes in a Postgres function for transactional integrity.

findings.title: ServiceChat save catch logs nothing
findings.detail: `.catch(() => { setSaving(false); addMessage('cadi', 'My fault — something didn't save…'); })` — the error object is discarded entirely. The earlier `} catch {` at line 230 (in a parser) also discards. In production this means a saveService failure (RLS, constraint, network) leaves zero trace for debugging.
findings.fix: `.catch(e => { console.error('[ServiceChat] save failed', e?.message, e?.code, e); ... })`. Same for line 230.

findings.title: PricingCalculator QuoteSummary catch swallows save error
findings.detail: `} catch {` with no logging in the save handler — same pattern. User sees an error status but you get no diagnostics.
findings.fix: Capture and log `e?.message` plus an error code if Supabase, even if you don't show it to the user.

findings.title: localStorage flags used for first-visit and first-setup chat state
findings.detail: `cadi_services_chat_opened` and `cadi_services_visited` keys are global per browser, not per user. If two users share a browser (uncommon but happens for cleaning businesses where owner + admin share a laptop), the second user gets no onboarding chat. Also no cleanup on logout — values persist forever.
findings.fix: Either key by user.id (`cadi_services_chat_opened_${user.id}`) or store the flag on the profiles table (e.g. setup_data.services_chat_opened) so it follows the user.

findings.title: Three frequency vocabularies cannot be reconciled at the DB level
findings.detail: services table uses boolean columns frequency_one_off/weekly/fortnightly/monthly/quarterly/annually. pricing_rules.frequency_modifiers uses numeric keys one_off/weekly/fortnightly/four_weekly/monthly. So a service offered 'quarterly' has nowhere in pricing_rules to express a quarterly modifier; a pricing rule with a 'four_weekly' modifier maps to no service-level frequency flag. Front Desk quoting against this will silently fall through to base price or default modifiers.
findings.fix: Agree on canonical set (recommend: one_off, weekly, fortnightly, four_weekly, monthly, quarterly, annually). Migrate services table to a single jsonb `frequencies` column or add the missing booleans, and add the missing modifier slots to pricing_rules. Audit the quote engine for silent-fallback paths.

findings.title: AI chat history grows unbounded on every send
findings.detail: sendMessage builds `nextHistory = [...messages, newUserMsg]` and POSTs the entire transcript every turn. After 20-30 turns this is significant token spend per message on Haiku and slow on mobile. No truncation, no summarisation.
findings.fix: Cap at last N messages (e.g. 12) when building apiMessages; or summarise older turns into a single system note after every 10 messages.

findings.title: my_business_id() locks staff out and provides no role separation
findings.detail: my_business_id() resolves business via `businesses.owner_user_id = auth.uid()` only. Consequences: (a) staff/cleaners signed into the org return NULL and cannot read services at all (Front Desk/staff dashboards break for them); (b) there is NO role check distinguishing owner vs staff — any user whose auth.uid matches owner_user_id has full CRUD on services, pricing_rules, pricing_addons including read/write of `private_notes` (which the UI explicitly labels as owner-only at Services.jsx:822). Britannia (1500 PAYE staff) cannot enforce least privilege with this model.
findings.fix: Replace my_business_id() with a membership table (business_members(user_id, business_id, role)) and add per-policy role checks. Restrict UPDATE/DELETE and SELECT of `private_notes` to role in ('owner','manager'). Split RLS into per-action policies (SELECT vs INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE).

findings.title: `private_notes` exposed via the same RLS policy as quotable fields
findings.detail: UI tells the user 'Only you can see this' for private_notes (cost data, supplier costs, job memories). But the table has a single `for all using (business_id = my_business_id())` policy — any user the DB treats as the owner sees this column. There is no column-level grant, no view, and no role gate. If staff accounts are ever added to the same business in future (which the Britannia roadmap requires), this becomes a confidentiality breach versus the in-UI promise.
findings.fix: Move private_notes to a separate table services_private(business_id, service_id, notes) with a stricter policy, or build a `services_public` view that excludes the column for non-owner roles. Update the UI copy to match real behaviour until then.

findings.title: Free-text service fields (name, descriptions, pricing_notes, private_notes) sent to Anthropic as a third party without consent flow
findings.detail: User-typed content from the Services tab is forwarded to Anthropic (a US/EU sub-processor) through the parse-service-nlp and ai-generate edge functions. There is no consent banner, no sub-processor list shown to the user, and no opt-out. Under UK GDPR Art 13/14 + Art 28 the controller must (a) disclose Anthropic as a processor, (b) have a DPA in place, and (c) flag this in the privacy policy. private_notes may include staff/supplier names which is processed-data about identified individuals.
findings.fix: Add Anthropic to the public sub-processor list, add a one-time disclosure modal the first time chat is used, document the DPA, and strip private_notes from any payload that goes to the model (it is never needed for NLP parsing).

findings.title: Error and PII logging via console.error in production bundle
findings.detail: console.error calls run in production and surface in browser devtools / any front-end log shipper (Sentry/etc) that hooks console. The edge function logs Anthropic responses on parse failure (line 176 `console.error('Failed to parse Haiku response:', text)`) which can include the user's free-text input verbatim — this gets persisted in Supabase function logs (a separate retention surface). Under GDPR this is incidental processing that needs to be documented or scrubbed.
findings.fix: Route client errors through a single logger that redacts message bodies; in parse-service-nlp, log only the response status and a hash of the input, not the raw text.

findings.title: No DELETE / data-export hook for services data on account closure
findings.detail: services has `on delete cascade` from businesses, which covers account-deletion, but there is no user-facing export endpoint for the services menu + pricing data (UK GDPR Art 15 right of access, Art 20 portability). Pricing/service catalogue arguably qualifies as the controller's business data rather than personal data, but private_notes can include identifiable references (staff names, supplier contacts) so an export route is needed.
findings.fix: Add a 'Download my data' action that exports services + pricing_rules + pricing_addons as JSON/CSV, and document the deletion path in the privacy policy.

findings.title: Client trusts numeric inputs as parseFloat without bounds
findings.detail: numericField uses parseFloat with no max/precision/sign clamp. Negative prices, NaN/Infinity slip through to the DB as numeric. Not a security issue per se (RLS still scopes the row) but the DB has no CHECK constraints either — a typo creates negative quotes Front Desk will then offer to customers.
findings.fix: Add CHECK (price >= 0) on price columns in a migration; clamp client-side too.

findings.title: localStorage flags used for first-visit gating — trivially bypassable but not a security boundary
findings.detail: `cadi_services_chat_opened` and `cadi_services_visited` are stored in localStorage. Not exploitable, but worth noting the chat auto-trigger on first setup will re-fire if the user clears storage — not a privacy issue but flagged for completeness.
findings.fix: Move first-visit state to `onboarding_progress` or a profile column if it ever guards anything sensitive.

findings.title: No XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML — confirmed clean
findings.detail: Grep confirms zero dangerouslySetInnerHTML usage across the audited files. All user input rendered via React's text interpolation, which auto-escapes. No SQL string interpolation observed; all DB access goes via Supabase parameterised client methods. No PII in URLs (no IDs, no query strings carry customer/owner data on the Services tab).
findings.fix: No change required — keep the rule by adding an ESLint rule react/no-danger to lock it in.

findings.title: Market-rate pricing intelligence with local benchmarks
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: 'Check my prices' button on each service card AND a banner on Services tab when prices haven't been reviewed in 90+ days. Also surfaced inside ServiceChat when user proposes a price ('Is £18/hr right for my area?'). (b) Claude (Sonnet): given service type, postcode/region from business profile, frequency, duration, materials, and the user's customer base avg-job-value from invoices table — returns a recommended range with reasoning ('In LS postcodes, EOT cleans average £180-£240 for a 3-bed; you're at £150 which is 22% below median. Three options: raise to £195, add a deep-clean upsell tier, or keep as a market-entry loss leader'). Pulls from web search for competitor rates if user opts in. (c) Wow: competitors (Squeegee, Aworka, CleanGuru) ship static rate cards. Cadi gives a reasoned, postcode-aware recommendation that factors in the user's own job history. Plus it explains the 'why' — pricing psychology and competitor positioning. (d) Tables: services, business_profile (postcode), invoices (historical job values), customers (count/density by area). Optional: WebSearch tool for live competitor scraping. (e) M. (f) Sonnet — needs reasoning over multiple data sources and nuanced output.

findings.title: Profitability X-ray per service
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: 'Profit check' tab inside each service edit modal, plus a Services dashboard widget 'Your most/least profitable services'. (b) Claude (Sonnet): inputs are service definition (price, duration, materials cost, equipment notes), staff_payroll data (avg hourly cost incl. NI/pension), historical jobs from schedule for this service, supply_costs estimate. Output: gross margin %, break-even calculation, and prose like 'Your weekly domestic clean is technically £25/hr revenue but after Sarah's £14.50 fully-loaded cost, travel time between jobs (avg 18min from postcode pattern), and £2.30 supplies — your real margin is £4.20/hr or 17%. Two of your services lose money once you factor travel.' Suggests price hikes, route batching, or service retirement. (c) Wow: nobody in cleaning SaaS does this. Field-service tools show revenue, not true unit economics. This is the killer 'oh shit' moment for owners. (d) Tables: services, staff (wage_rate, employment_type), schedule/jobs, invoices, payroll_runs, business_profile. Heavy cross-tab compounding — uses Staff + Schedule + Customers. (e) L. (f) Sonnet — multi-step reasoning, financial calculations, narrative output.

findings.title: AI service-menu gap detector from customer requests
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: passive banner 'You've turned away £X of work this quarter — see why' on Services tab. Click to see Claude's analysis. (b) Claude (Haiku for ingestion, Sonnet for synthesis): scans front_desk_messages, lost_leads table, calculator_quotes that didn't convert, and customer notes for service requests that don't match any active service ('carpet clean', 'oven', 'Airbnb turnaround', 'gutter clearance'). Outputs ranked list: 'You've had 14 requests for one-off deep cleans in the last 60 days and 9 for oven cleaning — neither is in your menu. Estimated missed revenue: £2,800. Want me to draft these services?' One-click adds them via ServiceChat. (c) Wow: turns Front Desk's passive lead capture into a strategic menu coach. Competitors require manual review of lost leads. (d) Tables: front_desk_messages, leads, calculator_quotes, customers, services. Compounds Front Desk + Calculator + Services. (e) M. (f) Haiku for the keyword extraction pass, Sonnet for synthesis and pricing suggestions.

findings.title: Frequency optimizer — push customers up the ladder
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: 'Upsell opportunities' card on Services tab and per-service 'Who could move to weekly?' button. (b) Claude (Sonnet): given a service like 'Fortnightly domestic' — scans customers on that service, their job history (cancellations, last-minute add-ons, complaint notes), and similar customers on weekly. Output: prioritized customer list with a tailored upsell message draft per customer ('Mrs Patel — fortnightly for 14 months, twice added oven clean last-minute, premium postcode. Suggested script: "We've noticed you've added extras the last two visits — would a weekly visit at £X save you hassle?"'). One-tap sends via SMS/email integration. (c) Wow: cleaning businesses leave huge ARPU on the table because owners don't have time to think customer-by-customer. Claude does it for them with personalized scripts. (d) Tables: customers, services, schedule, invoices, communications. Compounds Customers + Services + Schedule. (e) M. (f) Sonnet — needs nuanced customer analysis and persuasive copy.

findings.title: Dynamic description rewriter for conversion
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: 'Polish for Front Desk' button on each service description field, plus auto-suggest on save if description is <20 words or generic. Also surfaced as 'A/B test this service' on Services list — split-tests two descriptions on Front Desk and reports which converts. (b) Claude (Haiku for drafts, Sonnet for A/B analysis): inputs are raw description, service type, included/excluded items, target customer (from business_profile.brand_voice), and conversion data from Front Desk. Outputs 3 polished variants optimized for trust, urgency, or value framing — with reasoning per variant. (c) Wow: turns Services tab into a marketing tool. Competitors treat descriptions as plain text. (d) Tables: services, business_profile, front_desk_quotes (for conversion tracking). (e) S for rewriter, M for A/B layer. (f) Haiku for generation, Sonnet for the conversion-analysis layer.

findings.title: Conversational bulk pricing review ('Cadi, raise all my prices 8%')
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: extension of ServiceChat — natural language commands like 'put my exterior services up 5%', 'add a £15 weekend surcharge to all one-offs', 'create a winter discount on gutter clearance until March', 'match my prices to inflation since last review'. Also a 'Yearly price review' wizard launched from Services dashboard. (b) Claude (Sonnet): parses intent, identifies affected services, computes new prices, drafts a customer-comms email explaining the increase (UK-tone, references CPI if requested), and surfaces a preview/confirm step before writing. Stores price history for rollback. (c) Wow: cleaning owners dread price hikes — Claude handles the math, the comms, and the courage. Competitors require row-by-row editing. (d) Tables: services, price_history (new), customers (for comms list), business_profile. Add a service_price_history table. (e) M. (f) Sonnet — needs intent parsing + drafting + transactional safety.

findings.title: Auto-build full service menu from a competitor URL or photo
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: in ServiceChat first-setup AND a 'Import from competitor' button on empty Services tab. User pastes a competitor's website URL, a PDF price list, or even a photo of their old printed menu. (b) Claude (Sonnet + vision for photos, WebFetch for URLs): scrapes/OCRs the service list, normalizes into Cadi's schema (category, pricing_method, frequency, included/excluded), shows a diff/review screen ('Found 9 services. I've marked 3 as commercial — confirm?'), and bulk-creates with one click. For competitor scrape, suggests differentiators ('They charge per room, you could undercut with per_size hourly'). (c) Wow: removes the single biggest onboarding blocker for switchers from Squeegee/Aworka. Demo-day moment. (d) Tables: services, service_catalogue (templates), business_profile. Tools: WebFetch, Read (for PDFs/images). (e) M. (f) Sonnet with vision.

findings.title: Smart service bundling and package builder
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: 'Suggest bundles' on Services tab once user has 5+ services. Also appears in Calculator when customer adds two related services. (b) Claude (Sonnet): given full service menu + historical co-occurrence in invoices ('weekly clean + oven appears in 31% of quotes'), suggests named bundles ('Move-In Ready: deep clean + oven + carpet — £X with 12% saving'). Drafts marketing copy, sets bundle pricing logic, and creates the bundle as a service. (c) Wow: AOV uplift without owner thinking. Competitors have no concept of bundles built from data. (d) Tables: services, invoices (line items), calculator_quotes. Requires invoice_items granularity. (e) M. (f) Sonnet.

findings.title: Service health monitor — drift, gaps, and stale items
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: persistent 'Services health' badge on tab (green/amber/red). Click for Claude-generated audit. (b) Claude (Haiku for checks, Sonnet for narrative): runs nightly job checking — services with no jobs in 90 days, services with high cancellation rate, services missing pricing, services with descriptions shorter than menu siblings, services with margin below threshold, services priced identically (suggests differentiation). Returns a prioritized owner punch-list with one-tap fixes. (c) Wow: the Services tab gradually rots in every cleaning business. Nobody else nags about it with intelligence. (d) Tables: services, schedule, invoices, payroll. Cron job + edge function. (e) S for checks, M for the narrative layer. (f) Haiku for checks, Sonnet for the weekly digest.

findings.title: Quote-to-service feedback loop ('this quote wasn't in your menu')
findings.detail: (a) Trigger: automatic — every time PricingCalculator or Front Desk generates a quote using 'custom' pricing or manual overrides, Claude logs it and surfaces a Services-tab banner: 'You've manually quoted 7 jobs this month that don't match a service. Want me to formalize them?' (b) Claude (Sonnet): clusters the manual quotes ('5 of these are end-of-tenancy variations, 2 are post-construction'), proposes new services with median price + suggested description from the quote notes. One-click adds them to the menu. (c) Wow: the menu evolves with the business automatically. Owners typically realize 6 months later they've been quoting the same custom job 40 times. (d) Tables: calculator_quotes, front_desk_quotes, services. Cross-tab: Calculator + Front Desk + Services. (e) M. (f) Sonnet for clustering + naming.
